Tutorial: Visualization

GreenhousesJL includes two built-in visualization functions for inspecting simulation results and greenhouse geometry.

Setup

Load a Makie backend before GreenhousesJL:

using GLMakie          # interactive windows (mouse rotation for 3D)
using GreenhousesJL

For headless environments (CI, remote server), use CairoMakie instead, it produces the same images but without opening a window.

Time-series plots: plot_results

After running a simulation:

tmy = load_tmy("path/to/10Dec-22Nov.txt")
p = SavonaParams(tmy; with_walls=true)
u0 = initial_state(p; T0=278.15, T_soil0=276.15)
sol = solve(ODEProblem(greenhouse_rhs!, u0, (0.0, 604800.0), p),
            Tsit5(); reltol=1e-6, saveat=3600.0)

fig = plot_results(sol, p)

This opens an interactive window with three panels:

PanelLeft axisRight axis
TemperaturesT_air, T_cover, T_floor, T_wall, T_ext, setpoint [°C]—
WeatherGlobal solar irradiance [W/m²]Wind speed [m/s]
HeatingHeating power [W/m²]Cumulative energy [kWh]

The heating panel only appears when Kp > 0.

To save as PNG:

plot_results(sol, p; save_path="my_results.png")

3D geometry view: view_greenhouse_3d

view_greenhouse_3d(p)

This displays the greenhouse envelope in 3D:

  • Roof, transparent blue glass panels
  • Walls & gables, semi-transparent blue (slightly more opaque than roof)
  • Floor, brown slab
  • Ridge, red line (for asymmetric roofs)
  • Gutters, grey lines

With GLMakie, you can rotate the view by dragging with the mouse, zoom with scroll, and pan with right-click.

Savona vs generic

The function automatically detects the Savona 5×5 m geometry (floor area ≈ 25 m²) and renders the correct asymmetric 30°/60° roof. Any other floor area produces a generic rectangular box.

Comparing with and without walls

p_walls = SavonaParams(tmy; with_walls=true)
p_no    = SavonaParams(tmy; with_walls=false)

fig1 = view_greenhouse_3d(p_walls; save_path="with_walls.png")
fig2 = view_greenhouse_3d(p_no;    save_path="no_walls.png")

CSV export

Not a visualization per se, but useful for post-processing in Python, Excel, etc.:

export_csv("simulation.csv", sol, p)

Produces a clean CSV with one row per time step and columns for all temperatures, weather inputs, and heating variables. See API Reference for the full column list.
